speaker: interviewer
text: Today is January 23, 1996. Survivor Bronia Schwebel Sonnenschein, B-R-O-N-I-A; Schwebel, S-H-W-E-B-E-L; Sonnenschein, S-O-N-N-E-N-S-C-H-E-I-N. Interviewer, Victoria Zaric-Hartman-- V-I-C-T-D-R-I-A, Z-A-R-I-C; Hartman, H-A-R-T-M-A-N. In the city of Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada. It is interviewed in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera's rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is January 23, 1996. Survivor Bronia Schwebel Sonnenschein. I'm the interviewer, Victoria Zaric-Hartman, in the city of Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada. The interview is going to be in English language.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera's rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us what is your name? And if you will spell for us, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Bronia Sonnenschein, nee Schwebel. And?

speaker: interviewer
text: If you can spell it for us, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Bronia, B-R-O-N-I-A; Sonnenschein-- S-O-N-N-E-N-S-C-H-E-I-N. My maiden name Schwebel, S-C-H-W-E-B-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any other names that you go by?

speaker: interviewee
text: Briefly when I was married in the ghetto, my name was Strauss, S-T-R-A-U-S-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any nicknames that people called you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never liked nicknames.

speaker: interviewer
text: The city and the country where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in a city, Zloczow. It was in Galicia. I've no recollection. I came to Vienna when I was about four months old with my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: You grew up in Vienna. Did you have any siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: A sister three years younger. Her name was Paula, married name Lenga, L-E-N-G-A; maiden name Schwebel, like I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother-- what was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eh, Kawalek, K-A-W-A-L-E-K-- Amelia Kawalek.

speaker: interviewer
text: And father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Abraham Schwebel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any uncles and aunts?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had numerous uncles on my father's side, on my mother's side. My father's family I didn't know that well. They lived in Romania. It is no distance nowadays in Europe. In those days, it was a distance.

speaker: interviewee
text: So if I remember-- fondly remember-- it was my mother's brothers and sisters and my maternal grandmother that I adored. Unfortunately, I don't remember my grandfather, who died when I was four years old. Do you recall any one of your relatives that you can actually remember, interacting, being with them--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I--

speaker: interviewer
text: --associating?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were all the time together. We were such a close-knit family. We all lived in Vienna, not far from each other. And, um, I loved them all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you recall any of the names and the date of births?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's younger sister-- I refer to her because she lived for the last 20 years in Vancouver. Her name was Anne Morse, M-O-R-S-E. Her husband was Alfred. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I will talk about her because we had the closest-- lived here together for 20 years. As I say, she was the younger sister of my mother. She had no children, and she loved us dearly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, um, how old she was, uh, when you were as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't remember her wedding, but she was married when I was a child. But I really do not know the year. I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where she was born and her--

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in Husiatyn. That is also Galicia. That I know. And, uh, I think she even came to Vienna before. But that's the only things that I remember because my mother told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, any other cousins or uncles or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had cousins and uncles. My mother had three brothers-- Jakob; Carla, the youngest; and Zuma. I don't know what the German equivalent is. And I truly-- I was very, very much attached to them. My-- the oldest brother died on the streets of Vienna when the deportation started-- heart-- fatal heart attack. I always thought it was good. I knew after what I went through he wouldn't have survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us a little bit about your childhood? How did you-- um, when you went to school-- let's say start from kindergarten and up.

speaker: interviewee
text: I do. I do, Victoria. I had the happiest childhood you can imagine, not because we were very rich people. We were average, middle class. I want to tell you what our family was. I want to quote my son. I had overheard him when he was five years old talking to his friends. I don't know what made them do those things, but that's what he said when he was five years old-- we are not rich, we are not poor, but we're a happy family. I can say the same. We were a very happy family. I remember-- you asked kindergarten. I remember kindergarten only one incident. I went to a French kindergarten, French-German. My mother language was German-- Austrian. And shortly after I came home, and the whole family was so proud of me because I could speak a few words French. This is the rhyme that I recited. [SPEAKING FRENCH AND GERMAN] I do not know why I remember it so clearly, but it made an impression on my parents, on the family. I was declared a wunderkind. This is the only little incidence that I clearly remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, the-- the poem that you just recite-- in which language did you recite?

speaker: interviewee
text: In German. It was mixed German and French. It was a French kindergarten, so obviously it was both languages. One says it in German, the other is the-- in French and the other translation is German. But it was a big hit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how was your, um, religious life? Did, uh, your parents go to synagogue on a regular basis, or--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were observant the holidays. The Jewish holidays were beautifully celebrated in our house. Sometimes I think I can smell my mother's cooking-- the gefilte fish, the chicken soup. We observed the holidays in a beautiful way. Whether it was Hanukkah or Purim or Passover, Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, these were great events in the family. I went to public school. So did my sister. And we had, um, a religious training twice I week, I believe. A religious schoolteacher came and taught us Jewish history and Jewish customs and Jewish songs. And talking of songs, my mother played the piano beautifully. And she played beautifully Jewish songs, Jewish lullabies. And I remember them, too. And it was a very nice Jewish home. We were not observing as far as kosher is concerned. We were not. But in our hearts, we really knew where our loyalties are, where we belong.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Shabbat a very special day for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbat, it-- all the holy days were special. Shabbat-- when I started to go to school, a little bit older than elementary, we had to attend every Saturday morning a junior congregation. It was also close to where we lived. And this I remember. But it wasn't a big strain because I went with my friends. To me it was a lot of fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she going to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm glad you ask about my sister. I had only one sister. But she was the best sister. In later years, she became my very best friend. And the day she passed away-- it will be 10 years February 6-- was just about the saddest day. And I'm not lying if I tell you I miss her every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we go back to-- to childhood, your mom and dad, what did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had, before he was married, or even maybe when he was married, uh, had studied law in-- today it's called Lviv. In German, it was called Lemberg. It was also in Galicia. In the parts that they lived-- it was called in Austria and Poland-- the 'intelligentsia,' quote unquote, spoke German. But when he later came to Vienna, he-- it was difficult to practice law, and so he went into business with two of my uncles, my mother's brothers-- textile. And my mother was a Jewish mother. She was at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was at home raising the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Devoting her life to her home, to her children. She was strict. When my mother said no, you knew you didn't have a chance. But she was the best.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything from the-- from the-- going to synagogues, any songs, any-- any reciting, anything that left memorable thoughts on your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: There is one that will stick out in my memory til the day I die. As I say, I was in public school, but the teacher that taught us religion, it was Purim, and she wrote a play for Purim. Now, when I went to school in Vienna-- I don't know if has changed-- we were not boys and girls together-- nobody-- nobody. It was strictly separated. So obviously the girls had to play boys' roles. My best friend, Mary Anna-- she was a beautiful girl with long, brown braids-- was obviously Queen Esther. How I was chosen to play Mordecai, I'm not quite sure. Anyway, my mother gave me a sheet, white sheet, and somehow a golden belt and, of course, a grey beard. And the words that I recited as Mordecai have followed me all my life. I'd like to recite them for you here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please do.

speaker: interviewee
text: I recited them in German, and coming to Canada I never I had a script, but I wrote it down for my children and later grandchildren. In German it was-- [SPEAKING GERMAN] The English is, 'And if in the--' I better do it from here. 'And if in the distant future a Haman should rise again, bringing death and destruction to our people, then may the memory of this day give us hope and courage in our hours of danger. And may we never lose the conviction that God will be always with us, as He was with us today.'

speaker: interviewer
text: How much of an impact that poem had unto you throughout the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not think as a child I realized the impact, but it must have left an incredible mark on me, because when Hitler marched into Vienna, this poem all of a sudden-- it was many years later. I was only 10 when I recited it. I was a young adult when Hitler came. I remembered it. I-- I don't know what power made me do to remember this poem. I remembered it all through our years during the Holocaust. I passed it on to my children. I passed it on to my grandchildren. And when I visited a Jewish school in Vancouver, the Talmud Torah Academy, I passed it on to those children, as well as in Camp Hatikvah. And maybe the incredible belief in God was maybe also instrumental, that I came through it. There must be a higher power.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe just a little bit for us what was like Friday night, waiting for Shabbat in your family? Lighting the candles?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not want to disappoint you. We didn't celebrate Friday nights too much. And, uh, we continued going where we wanted to go, play in the park or whatever we did, even on Shabbat.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, uh, I always believed these are outward signs. What's in your heart, this is really the most important thing. I never changed my name, I've never changed my religion, and strange-- when I once talked in the school, one student-- she was an Asian girl-- came up to me with tears in her eyes. She felt sorry for me, and she said, don't you think if you would have converted and believed in Jesus Christ you would be happier?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, but I'm not unhappy. I'm really very proud of who I am. I would never change it in my life. This was important to me and to my family as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, as you were growing up in a happy family, suddenly the war was coming in. Were your family not prepared for the war? Did they tell you anything, what to expect?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I must say, we knew what was going on across the way in Germany. Because Hitler came to power in 1933 in Germany. That should have been an indication that we are not safe, either. I don't know. Nobody-- I'm not talking about my family, my friends, my acquaintances, but nobody in Vienna thought any reason to leave. Maybe the odd one I didn't know they were, but 100,000 Jews in Vienna. But the majority, the vast majority, did not make arrangements to leave. And when Hitler marched in March 13, 1938, and we started to make arrangements, it was too late. The doors of the free world-- the free world has to be a lot to be ashamed of-- were closed. They were closed hermetically. They were sealed off. They closed their eyes. They closed their ears. And most definitely they closed their hearts. Because not so many should have died. Had they let in a little bit, a few, opened the door a crack, so many would have been saved. We tried. God knows, we tried.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were not prepared, did not come out in time. Your mother and father-- did they continue on during the first part of the-- of the year of the war-- did they continue working and having--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it wasn't the war. I want to refer you that it was 1938. And first we had to obey all these laws that Hitler introduced. We couldn't walk on the sidewalk. We could, but then a German-- a person-- a German or a uniform especially approached, we had to step down on the road and let him pass or her. We couldn't sit on any benches. Every bench had design in-- printed in oil-- 'Jews forbidden.' We had to wear the Star of David, left-- on the shoulder in front and in the back. The Nazis referred to it as a sign of shame. I didn't see it as a mark of shame. I actually realized I have nothing to be ashamed of. I have nothing to be ashamed of being Jewish-- on the contrary. Who should have been ashamed was the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: So wearing the Star of David did not bother me at all. It was done so that we are easily identified. Like I had met a lot of blond, blue-eyed people, but make no mistake, there is a Jew here. So that was our very visible identification. But let me ask you this. What would happened if you didn't wear the Star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I would have been greatly punished. And I would have admitted that I'm ashamed, besides being greatly punished. One law followed the other. It is hard to believe that they rounded up, mostly in the beginning, men on the street to scrub the streets with a toothbrush. And while we are bending down, the Nazis were free to spit on our head. And when my father came home after having been called and made to scrub the trees, he was a broken man.

speaker: interviewer
text: So even though punishment, no punishment--

speaker: interviewee
text: Punishment upon punishment.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a pride to be Jewish girl--

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: It has never left me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you wore that with pride.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wore it with pride. I never attached to it the word 'shame.' They called it a 'badge of shame.' To me it was a-- a badge of honor-- absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your mother and your father and your sister react to wearing the Star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody felt ashamed about it. It was really plain to see who was to be-- who should be ashamed-- definitely not us, the Jewish people. It put-- you got more scared because from far on the street you could see, point out, there's a Jew walking. But, uh, no. With us, they didn't accomplish that. They put the shame on themselves. Hitler came in March 1938. In November-- November 9, 1938, a government-incited riot took place. It was the idea of the government. The streets-- all the stores of the Jewish-- the-- the windows of the stores of Jewish people were smashed. Before, they had all to have a sign painted on it, 'Jew.' They were smashed, the glass littering the street. That's why they came up to call it Kristallnacht, the Night of the Broken Glass. All our synagogues went up in flames, and Jews were rounded up and taken to Dachau. In some cases, nobody can believe the cruelty. They sometimes sent home a package containing the ashes and requesting to whoever got the box with ashes of their loved one to pay five schilling. A, Jews don't believe in cremation. B, you really never knew whether those were the ashes of your loved one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you--

speaker: interviewee
text: But you had to pay up to get it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Out of all this, what you said, how much of it you witnessed yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: All of it. All of it. I was on the street running home, running home, like for my life. And there we sat, huddled together. And from the window, you could see the flames rising of the synagogues.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone send a package of a dead person, ashes, to your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because my father wasn't taken away. You could only do that when you took somebody away and then say, these the ashes, right? But after Kristallnacht, men were mostly rounded up and taken to Dachau concentration camp. We begged our father, please, you have to leave. We want you alive. He didn't want to leave us behind, but it really-- women were not in imminent danger at that time. My father had connection. He traveled a lot in his-- for his, uh, textile factory. He had great connection. I have to drink-- [DRINKS]-- with the city of Lodz. I hate Lodz. Everything bad came there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do spell Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-O-D-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- not, that was in Polish, and the Germans renamed it Litzmannstadt. My father had great connection, because Lodz was the Manchester of Poland, textile. And he knew very many people that had-- did well and did feel very prominent. He managed to get out. And by going out, he had a chance to maybe make us go out. Because if we would all huddle together, nobody would have gotten out, and he would have been taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you say that he got out--

speaker: interviewee
text: To Poland-- illegally.

speaker: interviewer
text: So illegally from Germany--

speaker: interviewee
text: From-- from Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Austria he went to Poland illegally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tried to see if he can get you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. So he went to-- to talk to the people he knew quite well. And they still were far removed from Hitler and from-- and it was Poland. And we were left behind. And it was difficult to cope. You always looked over your shoulder. You were afraid to go out. We had somebody that had, downstairs where we lived, a store. And it was a good person. He came up when it was dark and brought us milk and butter and bread. And he helped us a great deal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now we're going to stop for a minute to change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll come back, and we will continue on to see what's happened after that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.
